Alright, welcome back. So here's the deal. Before I dig into this idea of what exactly is a straight line script, I just want to reiterate one important point. That is the difference between experts and novices in terms of how they present ideas. See, here's the deal. As consumers, as human beings, we know when we're speaking to an expert and when we're speaking to a novice. We just know unconsciously, we feel it, we've got this something different about the two. I mean, here's the deal. Experts, they say a lot of smart shit. Sometimes they'll say some bullshit and occasionally they'll kind of shoot the shit with you, right? But what they never do is say stupid shit. That is reserved for novices. That's what it's deemed on a only novices have. You get it? And here's the deal. When it comes to selling, comes to making money, the experts are the one that end up closing the deal and the novices are the ones who don't. But there's one distinction. It's people who sound like an expert close the deal. Not who necessarily are. Now, again, I'm not saying I don't want you to become an expert in your field, but I know many, many experts who literally cannot close a door. They can't close because they are experts. They don't sound like experts. Conversely, I've trained many people who started off as novices, but because they've mastered the straight line system and had an awesome script, they sounded like experts. And that's the distinction to actually teach yourself, to train yourself to sound like an expert and at the heart of it are straight line scripts. So with that in mind, what actually is a straight line script? Well, basically what a script is a straight line script, it's the well thought out essence of the perfect sale. And if you can go back in time to the best 10 or 15 presentations you ever made, when did you sound the most awesome during the open? When did you sound great during your intelligence gallery phase? What are the questions exactly in the right order? When did you make that killer main body of your presentation where your airtight logical case was so airtight, it was that scream from the hilltops airtight logical case? When were you like that? And when were you closing like just an animal, your looping patterns are perfect, your tonality, you were just on. Imagine going through all those past presentations and even things that you've heard from other people you never used before. Imagine all of that stuff wrapped up into one cohesive package. That's what I mean by a perfect straight line script. Okay, now there are a ton of benefits obviously for using a straight line script. Let me give you seven of them right now, the seven main ones right now one by one and we'll dig into them afterwards. The other one's an obvious one. If the sale drags on, which it typically does past a few minutes, you don't run out of intelligence, things to say because you've already scripted out that eventuality. In other words, you can go on and on without saying that good old stupid SHIT, you could say shit and bleep me out. So that's the first point obvious but it's very, very important. It allows you to continue to sound like an expert. The reason when the sale goes on and on and typically happens without a script is you start to reveal yourself as a novice because you can't keep going back and forth back and go up and stay on point. That's number one. Number two, a series of straight line scripts end to end. What it does is it allows you to always be applying the right tonality at the right time. In other words, you can go beforehand and say, okay, here's my script. Here's what I got to say. What tonality should I use here? What should I use there? Now, here's the deal. When you're first getting started, this is crucial. When you're using tonality and you're consciously competent. Once you're unconsciously competent, you won't need that anymore. You'll just naturally say the right thing at the right time. But for many of you, when you're getting started, having a script allows you to essentially just apply the right tonality every moment you're talking to someone and that is just absolutely crucial. Okay, makes sense? Good. Let's move on. Let's move on to number three. An obvious one again, but it's important to point out, right? By planning out what you say beforehand, it ensures that you're using the best possible words, the best possible phrases. In other words, if you just try to wing it, I mean, how can you ensure you're really going to say the right things? The best is always the best way to say something. And I talk about that with a straight line is being the truth well told. There's many ways to describe your company. There's many ways to describe the product you sell. And there's many ways to talk about yourself each of the three tens, right? But there's one way that's the best of all. It just sounds better. It has some alliterations in there. It's just paste the right way and it makes you sound awesome. So there's two options here. Either option A, you could try to kind of wing it, right? And you do not have to pay to hope for the best or you could spend the time to prepare yourself. You have to be strategic preparation and ensure that you're always using the best possible words from start to finish. And obviously this also goes into sound like an expert. Make sense? Good. Okay. Number four, because now you've done this beforehand. So you've kind of done the whole conscious, the logical part. You've scripted it out. You know exactly what you're going to say before you say it. Well, guess what happens? It now frees up your conscious mind with its limited processing power. So you can now focus all of your attention on what your prospect is doing. How are they reacting to what you say? What is their tonality like? What's their, their, what is their active listening? How are they listening to you actively? You get these great unconscious cues. You could see when your prospect is in deep foreview and if they're starting to fall out of rapport and you can either slow down and go backwards, right? So it allows you to really gauge where your prospect was at in terms of his level of certainty and just if he's in rapport view. Okay. Very important. Next. Now this is probably the most crucial one of all, at least in my book. And that is by taking the time to really think things out. It allows you to come up with that scream from the hilltops airtight logical case. In other words, at the essence of all great selling of the straight line, right? It's like making that incredibly airtight logical case. I always say that what I want to do when I'm done with my main presentation and you know, in a loop once, right? I really filled in the frame so to speak. We'll get to that later, right? I want the prospect saying, wow, that's, that's a great product. And I love, I'm buying it right now. And those, they're not ready to buy. They're saying, whoo, that's a great product, right? That's the level of sort of they want to get them to about their product. Now with a script, I can prepare that beforehand. They can really think it through and make sure I come up with the absolute best words, the best language patterns possible to ensure that eventuality. Okay? So it's absolutely crucial. That's number five. Number six is a big one for, especially if you're a sales manager or an owner, right? It allows the management of script to essentially systemize the sales force. See, without a script, everybody says things a different way, does things a different way. And that's especially true if your people are out in the field and you can't supervise them. But either way, without a script, you just set yourself to like really painful growth. Because if you're not essentially controlling what people say, then how can you duplicate that going forward? It's very hard to expand beyond being a mom and pop if at the heart of the sales operation, you don't have a series of cohesive straight line scripts that allow you to grow with some uniformity and control to make sure that an office in Dallas is saying the same thing as an office in Prague, and same thing as an office in Sydney, or even within your office, that the guy on desk four is not saying something different than the girl on desk six. And trust me, I've seen this happen and it is impossible to grow and expand the company without systemizing it through the use of scripts. Okay? Number seven, and the last one of all is regulatory problems. In other words, in the absence of using scripts, what ends up happening is that salesmen tend to go off the reservation and say things that might get you into a lot of trouble. And this is not just for management. It's also just for the salesman. You don't want to do, you don't want to open yourself up to that sort of problem, whether it's an arbitration or a customer complaint, right? On the internet, they say one bad thing about you, it lives forever. So here's the deal with that. Now very often in truth, salespeople don't mean to go off the reservation and cause problems. They just run out of intelligent, compliant things to say it in the moment they panic and they say something they should not have said. Now granted, there are those times as well when people are just bad apples, right? And they go off the straight line because they just want to. That's a different situation. Most of the time though, don't you fire those people by the way, right? But most of the time when you, the salesperson or the manager, you know, when you see someone go off the reservation, it just simply panicked and just said something out of fear of losing the sale. When you have a great script, a straight line script, it stops that from happening. So it's a protective mechanism for the company and ensures not just short term success, but long term success as well. All right? So those are seven very obvious benefits to using straight line scripts. And trust me, when you use them in the real world and have this stuff locked down, the difference it will make in your business will be truly staggering. All right? With that, let's stop. Let's go to a quiz right now, do your exercises and when you're done, I will see you in the next lesson. Thank you.
